01 Purpose of Wireshark

Wireshark is an open-source, cross-platform network protocol analyzer — commonly called a packet sniffer. It captures live network traffic or reads saved packet captures (.pcap / .pcapng files) and lets users inspect individual packets down to the byte level.

// CORE DEFINITION Wireshark works by putting the network interface into promiscuous mode, allowing it to capture all frames on the network segment — not just those addressed to the host machine.

What Wireshark is Used For

🛡 Security
THREAT ANALYSIS

Detect unauthorized traffic, malware C2 communications, ARP spoofing, and rogue devices on a network.

🔧 Debug
TROUBLESHOOTING

Diagnose slow connections, failed DNS resolutions, TCP retransmissions, and broken application sessions.

📚 Education
LEARNING PROTOCOLS

Observe how real protocols behave in the wild — TCP handshakes, DNS queries, HTTP requests in action.

📊 Perf
PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS

Measure latency, throughput, retransmissions, and packet loss to evaluate network performance.

SS #1 Wireshark Home Screen — Interface Selection
02 How to Approach a Packet Capture

A structured approach ensures you collect meaningful data without being overwhelmed by irrelevant traffic.

1
Define Your Goal
Know what you're looking for before opening Wireshark — debugging DNS, verifying a TLS handshake, or hunting unexpected outbound connections.
2
Choose the Right Interface
Go to Capture → Options. Select the interface that carries the traffic you need — Wi-Fi, Ethernet, or loopback.
3
Set a Capture Filter (Optional)
Capture filters use BPF syntax and run before capture — they reduce file size and noise. Example: port 53 for DNS only.
4
Start the Capture & Reproduce the Event
Click the blue shark fin or press Ctrl+E, then visit websites or run your application to generate traffic.
5
Stop & Apply Display Filters
Press Ctrl+E to stop. Then use display filters like dns, http, tcp.flags.syn == 1 to drill down.
6
Save the Capture
File → Save As → capture.pcap. Use pcap format for compatibility with PyShark and other tools.
SS #2 TShark Version Verified in CMD
SS #3 PyShark Installation Verified
SS #4 Live Packet Capture — All 3 Panes Visible
SS #5 DNS Display Filter Applied — Only DNS Packets Shown
SS #6 Saving Capture as capture.pcap
03 Protocol Deep Dive — DNS
SELECTED PROTOCOL

DNS — Domain Name System
RFC 1034 / RFC 1035  |  UDP/TCP Port 53

DNS is the internet's distributed naming system — the "phone book" of the internet. It translates human-readable domain names like www.google.com into machine-readable IP addresses. DNS uses UDP on port 53 for most queries and falls back to TCP on port 53 when responses exceed 512 bytes.

DNS Packet Structure

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DNS MESSAGE FORMAT │ ├──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Header (12 B) │ ID | Flags | QDCOUNT | ANCOUNT │ ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Question │ QNAME (domain) | QTYPE | QCLASS │ ├──────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Answer RR │ NAME | TYPE | CLASS | TTL | RDATA │ └──────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┘

Common DNS Record Types

TYPEPURPOSEEXAMPLE
AMaps hostname to IPv4 addressgoogle.com → 142.250.190.68
AAAAMaps hostname to IPv6 addressgoogle.com → 2607:f8b0:...
CNAMEAlias from one name to anotherwww → google.com
MXMail exchange serversgoogle.com → aspmx.l.google.com
PTRReverse DNS — IP to hostname68.190.250.142 → google.com
TXTArbitrary text (SPF, DKIM)"v=spf1 include:..."
SS #7 DNS Packet Details — Transaction ID, Flags & Query Expanded
// SECURITY NOTE DNS traffic is unencrypted by default. An attacker on the same network can see every domain you query. Modern alternatives include DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNS over TLS (DoT).
04 PyShark Script — DNS Filter

PyShark is a Python wrapper for TShark. The script below opens a .pcap file, filters for DNS traffic, and prints the Source IP and queried domain name for every DNS packet found.

import pyshark

def analyze_dns(pcap_file):
    cap = pyshark.FileCapture(pcap_file, display_filter='dns')

    print("=" * 55)
    print(f"{'DNS TRAFFIC ANALYSIS':^55}")
    print(f"File: {pcap_file}")
    print("=" * 55)
    print(f"{'#':<5} {'SOURCE IP':<20} {'QUERIED DOMAIN'}")
    print("-" * 55)

    packet_count = 0
    query_count = 0

    for packet in cap:
        packet_count += 1
        try:
            if hasattr(packet, 'dns'):
                dns_layer = packet.dns
                qry_name = None
                if hasattr(dns_layer, 'qry_name'):
                    qry_name = dns_layer.qry_name
                elif hasattr(dns_layer, 'dns_qry_name'):
                    qry_name = dns_layer.dns_qry_name
                if qry_name:
                    src_ip = packet.ip.src
                    query_count += 1
                    print(f"{query_count:<5} {src_ip:<20} {qry_name}")
        except AttributeError:
            pass

    cap.close()
    print("-" * 55)
    print(f"Total packets analyzed : {packet_count}")
    print(f"DNS queries found      : {query_count}")
    print("=" * 55)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    PCAP_FILE = "capture.pcap"
    analyze_dns(PCAP_FILE)

Script Output — Live Results

SS #8 Python Script Output — DNS Queries (Top)
SS #9 Python Script Output — Total: 481 Packets / 481 DNS Queries Found
// RESULT The script successfully analyzed 481 packets from the capture file and identified 481 DNS queries — including domains such as chatgpt.com, google.com, wikipedia.com, stackoverflow.com, and chat.deepseek.com.
